-        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
+        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right
 
     return np.hstack([cleft, cright])

problem_statement.Title: separability_matrix incorrect for nested CompoundModel due to _cstack bottom-right assignment
problem_statement.Description: When computing separability for CompoundModel trees that use the '&' operator, nested subtrees that return a coord_matrix ndarray are not copied correctly into the bottom-right block of the composed coord_matrix. The _cstack operator (astropy/modeling/separable.py) assigns the scalar 1 into the bottom-right slice when right is an ndarray instead of copying the ndarray. This causes nested CompoundModel separability to differ from the equivalent flattened expression (reported example: Pix2Sky_TAN() & (Linear1D(10) & Linear1D(5)) gives a different matrix than Pix2Sky_TAN() & Linear1D(10) & Linear1D(5)).
problem_statement.RootCause: In astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack, when the right operand is an ndarray (i.e., the coord matrix produced by a nested subtree), the code fills the bottom-right block with the scalar 1 (cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1) instead of copying the right ndarray into that slice. This loses the nested subtree's 0/1 dependency pattern and forces the block to indicate every output depends on every input, producing incorrect separability for nested '&' compound models.
StepsToReproduce: from astropy.modeling import models as m
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5))  # expected: flattened pattern
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm)  # observed: bottom-right block is all True (incorrect)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Nested CompoundModels composed with '&' should produce the same separability matrix as the equivalent flattened expression. When a subtree produces a coord_matrix ndarray, that ndarray must be copied into the corresponding block of the composed coord_matrix, preserving its 0/1 dependency pattern.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When the right subtree is a nested CompoundModel (thus producing an ndarray), the bottom-right block of the composed coord_matrix is filled with scalar 1s, resulting in an incorrect separability matrix that indicates non-separable dependencies for outputs that are in fact independent.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) Fix astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack to copy the right ndarray into the bottom-right block instead of assigning scalar 1. 2) Optionally preserve dtype when allocating cleft/cright for ndarray operands to avoid upcasting. 3) Add a regression test in astropy/modeling/tests/test_separable.py that asserts a nested CompoundModel gives the same separability_matrix as the equivalent flattened expression (and include the simple 2x2 diagonal check for Linear1D & Linear1D). 4) Run the modeling test suite to ensure no other regressions.

patch:
diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
@@ -27,7 +27,6 @@ def get_fixedwidth_params(self, line):
 
 
 class SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData):
-    start_line = 3
     end_line = -1
     splitter_class = FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter
 
@@ -39,12 +38,29 @@ class RST(FixedWidth):
 
     Example::
 
-        ==== ===== ======
-        Col1  Col2  Col3
-        ==== ===== ======
-          1    2.3  Hello
-          2    4.5  Worlds
-        ==== ===== ======
+      >>> from astropy.table import QTable
+      >>> import astropy.units as u
+      >>> import sys
+      >>> tbl = QTable({"wave": [350, 950] * u.nm, "response": [0.7, 1.2] * u.count})
+      >>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.rst")
+      ===== ========
+       wave response
+      ===== ========
+      350.0      0.7
+      950.0      1.2
+      ===== ========
+
+    Like other fixed-width formats, when writing a table you can provide ``header_rows``
+    to specify a list of table rows to output as the header.  For example::
+
+      >>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.rst", header_rows=['name', 'unit'])
+      ===== ========
+       wave response
+         nm       ct
+      ===== ========
+      350.0      0.7
+      950.0      1.2
+      ===== ========
 
     Currently there is no support for reading tables which utilize continuation lines,
     or for ones which define column spans through the use of an additional
@@ -57,10 +73,15 @@ class RST(FixedWidth):
     data_class = SimpleRSTData
     header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
 
-    def __init__(self):
-        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)
+    def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
+        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
 
     def write(self, lines):
         lines = super().write(lines)
-        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
+        idx = len(self.header.header_rows)
+        lines = [lines[idx]] + lines + [lines[idx]]
         return lines
+
+    def read(self, table):
+        self.data.start_line = 2 + len(self.header.header_rows)
+        return super().read(table)

 ==== ========= ==== ====

problem_statement.Title: ascii.rst writer: TypeError on header_rows and incorrect border placement for multi-row headers
problem_statement.Description: When calling the RestructuredText (ascii.rst) writer with header_rows provided (for example header_rows=["name","unit"]) the writer raises a TypeError because RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows and therefore does not forward it to the FixedWidth base class. In addition, the RST.write implementation assumes the border/position line is always at index 1 in the list of lines returned by the base writer; this breaks when header_rows length != 1 and produces incorrect table borders. Tests covering ascii.rst with header_rows are missing.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) RST.__init__ has no header_rows parameter and does not forward header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__, causing a TypeError when core._get_writer passes header_rows through writer kwargs. 2) RST.write unconditionally uses lines[1] as the border/position line; that index is only correct for the default single header row. 3) SimpleRSTData.start_line is a hard-coded class attribute (3), so FixedWidth.__init__ will not override it when header_rows is supplied; this means data start offsets won't automatically adapt for non-default header_rows unless RST adjusts data.start_line when header_rows is explicitly provided. 4) There are no tests asserting ascii.rst writing/reading with header_rows variations.
StepsToReproduce: Run the Python snippet from the original report:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The ascii.rst writer should accept header_rows (default None) and forward it to FixedWidth so header/header_rows and data.header_rows are set consistently. When header_rows is provided (e.g. ['name','unit']), the writer should produce a valid reStructuredText simple-table with both name and unit header rows, with the top/bottom border (position line) placed at the correct index relative to the header rows. No TypeError should be raised.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling tbl.write(..., format='ascii.rst', header_rows=[...]) raises TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'. Even after addressing the TypeError, produced border lines may be placed incorrectly when header_rows length != 1 because RST.write assumes a fixed index for the border line.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) Modify RST.__init__ to accept header_rows=None and forward header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__. 2) After calling super().__init__, if the caller explicitly provided header_rows, update self.data.start_line so data rows start at the expected offset relative to the number of header rows and presence of a position_line. 3) Update RST.write to compute the index of the border/position line dynamically from header.header_rows and header.position_line (instead of using lines[1]). 4) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_rst.py that exercise writing (and read/write round-trips) with header_rows=['name','unit'] (including the QTable+units example) and assert the produced reStructuredText simple-table matches the expected layout. 5) Review SimpleRSTHeader.position_line and start_line offsets via tests to ensure read/write symmetry for non-default header_rows; adjust if tests show mismatches. These changes are minimal, align RST with other FixedWidth subclasses, and prevent TypeError and malformed output for multi-row headers.

diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
@@ -68,7 +68,7 @@ def _line_type(line, delimiter=None):
     _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
     _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
     _type_re = rf"^\s*((?P<command>{_command_re})|(?P<new>{_new_re})|(?P<data>{_data_re})?\s*(\!(?P<comment>.*))?\s*$"
-    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
+    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)
     line = line.strip()
     if not line:
         return "comment"
@@ -306,7 +306,7 @@ def _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None):
 
             values = []
             for v in line.split(delimiter):
-                if v == "NO":
+                if v.upper() == "NO":
                     values.append(np.ma.masked)
                 else:
                     # Understand if number is int or float

     example_qdp = """
     ! Swift/XRT hardness ratio of trigger: XXXX, name: BUBU X-2
     ! Columns are as labelled
@@ -70,6 +81,8 @@ def test_roundtrip(tmp_path):
     53000.123456 2.37847222222222e-05    -2.37847222222222e-05   -0.292553       -0.374935
     NO 1.14467592592593e-05    -1.14467592592593e-05   0.000000        NO
     """
+    if lowercase:
+        example_qdp = lowercase_header(example_qdp)
 
     path = str(tmp_path / "test.qdp")
     path2 = str(tmp_path / "test2.qdp")

problem_statement.Title: ascii.qdp: QDP reader should accept case-insensitive commands (e.g. 'read serr')
problem_statement.Description: The QDP ASCII reader (astropy.io.ascii.qdp) treats QDP command lines (for example "READ SERR 1 2") as case-sensitive. The line-classification regex in _line_type only matches uppercase commands, so lowercase or mixed-case commands such as "read serr 1 2" are not recognized and raise ValueError: "Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2". The downstream code expects to be able to lowercase tokens (e.g. command[1].lower()), so the core issue is only the case-sensitive detection of command lines. Additionally, data token detection for masked values uses an exact, case-sensitive comparison (if v == "NO"), which would miss lowercase 'no' tokens if present.
problem_statement.RootCause: The regex used to classify QDP lines in _line_type is compiled without the re.IGNORECASE flag, while _command_re contains literal uppercase tokens (e.g. 'READ [TS]ERR(...)'). As a result, command lines in lowercase or mixed case fail to match the 'command' branch and are treated as unrecognized lines. Separately, value-level masked-token detection in _get_tables_from_qdp_file compares tokens to the uppercase string "NO" exactly, so lowercase 'no' tokens are not converted to masked values.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file test.qdp with contents:
Observe ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Table.read(..., format='ascii.qdp') should accept QDP command lines regardless of case (e.g. 'READ SERR', 'read serr', 'Read Serr') and parse the error columns. Optionally, data tokens representing masked values (e.g. 'NO') should be recognized case-insensitively if the project policy prefers that.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Lowercase or mixed-case QDP command lines (e.g. 'read serr 1 2') are not recognized by the reader and cause a ValueError: 'Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2'.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) Minimal, local fix: compile the combined line-type regex in _line_type with re.IGNORECASE so command lines are matched case-insensitively. 2) Address the data-token masked-value gap by normalizing tokens in _get_tables_from_qdp_file before comparing to 'NO' (e.g., v_stripped.upper() == 'NO'). This keeps the scope local to the QDP reader and avoids broader changes in core._set_fill_values. 3) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py that include lowercase and mixed-case command lines (e.g., 'read serr 1 2') and optionally lowercase 'no' tokens to prevent regressions. 4) Update docstrings/doctests in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py to include examples with lowercase/mixed-case commands. 5) Consider (optional, larger-scope) whether core._set_fill_values should perform case-insensitive fill-value matching project-wide; if so, update core and readers and add tests accordingly. Recommended immediate changes are (1) and (2) above.

GitHub issues in the Astropy repository are used to track bug reports and feature requests; If your issue poses a question about how to use Astropy, please instead raise your question in the [Astropy Discourse user forum](https://community.openastronomy.org/c/astropy/8) and close this issue.

If you feel that this issue has not been responded to in a timely manner, please send a message directly to the [development mailing list](http://groups.google.com/group/astropy-dev).  If the issue is urgent or sensitive in nature (e.g., a security vulnerability) please send an e-mail directly to the private e-mail feedback@astropy.org.
Huh, so we do have this format... https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/io/ascii/index.html

@taldcroft , you know anything about this?
This is the format I'm using, which has the issue: https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.io.ascii.QDP.html

The issue is that the regex that searches for QDP commands is not case insensitive. 

This attached patch fixes the issue, but I'm sure there's a better way of doing it.

[qdp.patch](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/files/10667923/qdp.patch)

@jak574 - the fix is probably as simple as that. Would you like to put in a bugfix PR?

test_patch:
diff --git a/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py b/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
--- a/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
+++ b/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
@@ -1310,3 +1310,42 @@ def test_raise_method_not_supported():
     # raise error for unsupported propagation operations:
     with pytest.raises(ValueError):
         ndd1.uncertainty.propagate(np.mod, ndd2, result, correlation)
+
+
+def test_nddata_bitmask_arithmetic():
+    # NDData.mask is usually assumed to be boolean, but could be
+    # a bitmask. Ensure bitmask works:
+    array = np.array([[0, 1, 0], [1, 0, 1], [0, 1, 0]])
+    mask = np.array([[0, 1, 64], [8, 0, 1], [2, 1, 0]])
+
+    nref_nomask = NDDataRef(array)
+    nref_masked = NDDataRef(array, mask=mask)
+
+    # multiply no mask by constant (no mask * no mask)
+    assert nref_nomask.multiply(1.0, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask is None
+
+    # multiply no mask by itself (no mask * no mask)
+    assert nref_nomask.multiply(nref_nomask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask is None
+
+    # multiply masked by constant (mask * no mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(1.0, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # multiply masked by itself (mask * mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(nref_masked, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # multiply masked by no mask (mask * no mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(nref_nomask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # check bitwise logic still works
+    other_mask = np.array([[64, 1, 0], [2, 1, 0], [8, 0, 2]])
+    nref_mask_other = NDDataRef(array, mask=other_mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_mask_other.multiply(nref_masked, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask,
+        np.bitwise_or(mask, other_mask),
+    )

problem_statement.Title: NDDataRef mask propagation in v5.3: handle_mask called with None causing TypeError
problem_statement.Description: In astropy v5.3 the mask propagation for NDDataRef arithmetic regressed compared to v5.2. When doing arithmetic with handle_mask set to a numpy ufunc (e.g. np.bitwise_or) and exactly one operand has a mask, NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask can call the handle_mask callable with one argument equal to None. This raises a TypeError (e.g. unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'int' and 'NoneType'). The expected behaviour (and behaviour in v5.2) is that if only one operand has a mask that mask is copied to the result; handle_mask should only be invoked when both operands have non-None masks. The failure is reproducible using NDDataRef.multiply with a masked NDDataRef and a no-mask NDDataRef / scalar and handle_mask=np.bitwise_or.
problem_statement.RootCause: NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask is not symmetric in handling masks: it can call handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask) even when operand.mask is None. This causes numpy ufuncs (like np.bitwise_or) to be invoked with None as one argument, raising a TypeError. The code only had an explicit branch to copy operand.mask when self.mask is None, but lacked the symmetric branch to copy self.mask when operand.mask is None.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import numpy as np; from astropy.nddata import NDDataRef; arr = np.array([[0,1,0],[1,0,1],[0,1,0]]); mask = np.array([[0,1,64],[8,0,1],[2,1,0]]); n_nomask = NDDataRef(arr); n_mask = NDDataRef(arr, mask=mask); n_mask.multiply(1., handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask"
Also try the reverse ordering: n_nomask.multiply(n_mask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If only one operand has a mask, that mask should be copied (deepcopied) to the result. handle_mask should only be invoked when both operands have non-None masks. No TypeError should be raised when handle_mask is a numpy ufunc and one operand lacks a mask.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling arithmetic with handle_mask=np.bitwise_or and one operand lacking a mask raises TypeError (e.g. unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'int' and 'NoneType') because handle_mask is called with a None mask argument.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) Change NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask to explicitly handle the four cases: (a) handle_mask is None -> return None; (b) operand is None (collapse) -> deepcopy(self.mask); (c) both masks None -> return None; (d) exactly one mask present -> deepcopy(that mask); (e) both present -> call handle_mask(mask_self, mask_operand, **kwds). 2) Add unit tests to astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py that reproduce the regression and assert correct mask copying for the cases (masked * scalar), (masked * nomask), and (nomask * masked), using numpy ufuncs such as np.bitwise_or and np.logical_or. Tests should cover NDDataRef and likewise NDDataArray (or similar NDData subclasses) where applicable to prevent regressions. 3) Consider backporting the fix to any maintenance branches where v5.2->v5.3 regressions are being tracked.

hints_text:
Welcome to Astropy 👋 and thank you for your first issue!

A project member will respond to you as soon as possible; in the meantime, please double-check the [guidelines for submitting issues](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#reporting-issues) and make sure you've provided the requested details.

GitHub issues in the Astropy repository are used to track bug reports and feature requests; If your issue poses a question about how to use Astropy, please instead raise your question in the [Astropy Discourse user forum](https://community.openastronomy.org/c/astropy/8) and close this issue.

If you feel that this issue has not been responded to in a timely manner, please send a message directly to the [development mailing list](http://groups.google.com/group/astropy-dev).  If the issue is urgent or sensitive in nature (e.g., a security vulnerability) please send an e-mail directly to the private e-mail feedback@astropy.org.
@bmorris3 , do you think this is related to that nddata feature you added in v5.3?
Hi @KathleenLabrie. I'm not sure this is a bug, because as far as I can tell the `mask` in NDData is assumed to be boolean: 

https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/83f6f002fb11853eacb689781d366be6aa170e0e/astropy/nddata/nddata.py#L51-L55

There are updates to the propagation logic in v5.3 that allow for more flexible and customizable mask propagation, see discussion in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/14175.

You're using the `bitwise_or` operation, which is different from the default `logical_or` operation in important ways. I tested your example using `logical_or` and it worked as expected, with the caveat that your mask becomes booleans with `True` for non-zero initial mask values.
We are doing data reduction.  The nature of the "badness" of each pixel matters.  True or False does not cut it.  That why we need bits.  This is scientifically required.   A saturated pixel is different from a non-linear pixel, different from an unilliminated pixels, different .... etc. 

I don't see why a feature that had been there for a long time was removed without even a deprecation warning.
BTW, I still think that something is broken, eg.
```
>>> bmask = np.array([[True, False, False], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]])
>>> nref_bmask = NDDataRef(array, mask=bmask)
>>> nref_bmask.multiply(1.).mask
array([[True, None, None],
       [None, True, None],
       [None, None, True]], dtype=object)
```
Those `None`s should probably be `False`s not None's
There is *absolutely* a bug here. Here's a demonstration:

```
>>> data = np.arange(4).reshape(2,2)
>>> mask = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]]))
>>> nd1 = NDDataRef(data, mask=mask)
>>> nd2 = NDDataRef(data, mask=None)
>>> nd1.multiply(nd2, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or)
...Exception...
>>> nd2.multiply(nd1, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or)
NDDataRef([[0, 1],
           [4, 9]])
```

Multiplication is commutative and should still be here. In 5.2 the logic for arithmetic between two objects was that if one didn't have a `mask` or the `mask` was `None` then the output mask would be the `mask` of the other. That seems entirely sensible and I see no sensible argument for changing that. But in 5.3 the logic is that if the first operand has no mask then the output will be the mask of the second, but if the second operand has no mask then it sends both masks to the `handle_mask` function (instead of simply setting the output to the mask of the first as before).

Note that this has an unwanted effect *even if the masks are boolean*:
```
>>> bool_mask = mask.astype(bool)
>>> nd1 = NDDataRef(data, mask=bool_mask)
>>> nd2.multiply(nd1).mask
array([[False,  True],
       [ True, False]])
>>> nd1.multiply(nd2).mask
array([[None, True],
       [True, None]], dtype=object)
```
and, whoops, the `mask` isn't a nice happy numpy `bool` array anymore.

So it looks like somebody accidentally turned the lines

```
elif operand.mask is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)
```

into

```
elif operand is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)
```

@chris-simpson I agree that line you suggested above is the culprit, which was [changed here](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/commit/feeb716b7412c477c694648ee1e93be2c4a73700#diff-5057de973eaa1e5036a0bef89e618b1b03fd45a9c2952655abb656822f4ddc2aL458-R498). I've reverted that specific line in a local astropy branch and verified that the existing tests still pass, and the bitmask example from @KathleenLabrie works after that line is swapped. I'll make a PR to fix this today, with a new test to make sure that we don't break this again going forward. 
Many thanks for working on this, @bmorris3.

Regarding whether the `mask` is assumed to be Boolean, I had noticed in the past that some developers understood this to be the case, while others disagreed. When we discussed this back in 2016, however (as per the document you linked to in Slack), @eteq explained that the mask is just expected to be "truthy" in a NumPy sense of zero = False (unmasked) and non-zero = True (masked), which you'll see is consistent with the doc string you cited above, even if it's not entirely clear :slightly_frowning_face:.
Of course I think that flexibility is great, but I think intentional ambiguity in docs is risky when only one of the two cases is tested. 😬 
Indeed, I should probably have checked that there was a test for this upstream, since I was aware of some confusion; if only we could find more time to work on these important common bits that we depend on...

problem_statement.Title: io.fits: _scale_back_ascii no-op replace() call prevents 'E'->'D' exponent conversion
problem_statement.Description: In astropy/io/fits/fitsrec.py, FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii attempts to replace the exponent separator 'E' with 'D' for floating-point ASCII output by calling output_field.replace(encode_ascii('E'), encode_ascii('D')). numpy.char.replace / numpy.chararray.replace return a new array rather than modifying the existing array in-place. The code calls replace without assigning the returned array back into the existing buffer, so the substitution is a no-op and 'E' remains in the written ASCII output. encode_ascii returns bytes for a str input (b'E'), and elsewhere in the same module np.char.replace is used with assignment, indicating the intent is to use the returned array. This bug is isolated to FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii.
problem_statement.RootCause: The code assumes that numpy.chararray.replace (or np.char.replace) mutates the existing chararray in-place. In reality these functions return a new array; calling replace() without assigning the result does not alter output_field, so 'E' -> 'D' substitutions never take effect.
StepsToReproduce: Create an ASCII FITS table with a floating-point column whose format contains the 'D' exponent specifier (i.e. format uses 'D' as exponent separator).
Populate the column with a value that will be formatted with an exponent (e.g. 1.23e+04).
Write the table to an ASCII FITS file using astropy.io.fits.
Inspect the file contents: the exponent separator should be 'D' but will remain 'E' with the current code.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When writing ASCII table columns whose format indicates 'D' exponent notation, the output exponent separator characters in the written ASCII should be 'D' (e.g., 1.23D+04).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The code attempts to replace 'E' with 'D' by calling output_field.replace(...) but does not assign the return value; because replace returns a new array, the underlying buffer is not modified and exponent separators remain 'E'.
problem_statement.Suggestions:
Assign the result of np.char.replace back into the existing output_field buffer so the substitution takes effect without rebinding the view to a new array. For example, use:

    if 'D' in format:
        output_field[:] = np.char.replace(output_field, encode_ascii('E'), encode_ascii('D'))

Add a unit test that writes an ASCII FITS table with a 'D' format column and verifies the written file contains 'D' exponent separators. Ensure the assignment uses slice assignment (output_field[:]) so the underlying recarray buffer/view identity is preserved.

hints_text: It is tested with `astropy/io/fits/tests/test_checksum.py:test_ascii_table_data` but indeed the operation is not inplace and it does not fail. Using 'D' is probably better, but since #5362 (I had vague memory about something like this ^^, see also #5353) anyway 'D' and 'E' are read as double, so I think there is not difference on Astropy side.

patch:
diff --git a/astropy/wcs/wcs.py b/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
--- a/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
+++ b/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
@@ -1212,6 +1212,9 @@ def _array_converter(self, func, sky, *args, ra_dec_order=False):
         """
 
         def _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin):
+            if any([x.size == 0 for x in axes]):
+                return axes
+
             try:
                 axes = np.broadcast_arrays(*axes)
             except ValueError:
@@ -1235,6 +1238,8 @@ def _return_single_array(xy, origin):
                 raise ValueError(
                     "When providing two arguments, the array must be "
                     "of shape (N, {0})".format(self.naxis))
+            if 0 in xy.shape:
+                return xy
             if ra_dec_order and sky == 'input':
                 xy = self._denormalize_sky(xy)
             result = func(xy, origin)

diff --git a/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py b/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
--- a/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
+++ b/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
@@ -1093,3 +1093,21 @@ def test_keyedsip():
     assert isinstance( w.sip, wcs.Sip )
     assert w.sip.crpix[0] == 2048
     assert w.sip.crpix[1] == 1026
+
+
+def test_zero_size_input():
+    with fits.open(get_pkg_data_filename('data/sip.fits')) as f:
+        w = wcs.WCS(f[0].header)
+
+    inp = np.zeros((0, 2))
+    assert_array_equal(inp, w.all_pix2world(inp, 0))
+    assert_array_equal(inp, w.all_world2pix(inp, 0))
+
+    inp = [], [1]
+    result = w.all_pix2world([], [1], 0)
+    assert_array_equal(inp[0], result[0])
+    assert_array_equal(inp[1], result[1])
+
+    result = w.all_world2pix([], [1], 0)
+    assert_array_equal(inp[0], result[0])
+    assert_array_equal(inp[1], result[1])

problem_statement.Title: WCS transformations raise InconsistentAxisTypesError for zero-length inputs
problem_statement.Description: High-level WCS transformation methods (e.g. wcs_pix2world) raise an InconsistentAxisTypesError when called with empty coordinate inputs (zero-length lists/arrays). Traceback shows the error originates from the underlying wcslib C routines (wcsp2s / wcsp.s2p) because WCS._array_converter currently always calls into the C extension even when the prepared coordinate array has zero rows. The user-visible behavior should instead be to return empty arrays/lists shaped consistently with the broadcasted input axes and the public API calling style.
problem_statement.RootCause: WCS._array_converter's internal dispatch helpers (_return_list_of_arrays and _return_single_array) always call func(xy, origin) after preparing xy, even when xy has zero rows (xy.shape[0] == 0). Passing ncoord == 0 into the wcslib C routines triggers wcslib to raise InconsistentAxisTypesError. There is no short-circuit to return appropriately-shaped empty outputs in the Python layer.
StepsToReproduce: Observed: InconsistentAxisTypesError raised from wcslib (ncoord==0). Expected: empty outputs.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When called with zero-length coordinate inputs, WCS transformation methods should return empty arrays (or lists/tuples of empty arrays) whose shapes match the broadcasted input shapes (for the separate-axis/list-of-arrays calling style) or (0, M) where M is the number of output columns (for the single-array calling style). For the ra/dec special-case, preserve the existing distinction between list-of-arrays (tuple of two arrays) and single-array (Nx2 array) return conventions.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling wcs.wcs_pix2world([], [], 0) raises InconsistentAxisTypesError coming from wcslib because the Python dispatcher calls the C extension with ncoord == 0.
problem_statement.Suggestions: Implement early-return guards in WCS._array_converter (both list-of-arrays and single-array calling styles) to detect xy.shape[0] == 0 and return appropriately-shaped empty outputs without calling func(xy, origin). Also add an early-return in WCS._all_world2pix to handle receiving an empty pix0 from wcs_world2pix to avoid empty-array reductions. Update/add tests covering zero-length inputs for all public transformation methods. Audit any code/tests that call low-level wcslib methods directly (e.g. w.wcs.p2s / w.wcs.s2p) and update them to use the public WCS dispatchers or add guards, because those direct calls still pass zero-length arrays directly to the C extension and will still raise.
